author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Braised Chicken With Rosemary and Crispy Artichokes Recipe
commentBody: I halved this recipe, using chicken thighs cooked in a cast-iron Dutch oven. In place of fresh baby artichokes, I used frozen artichoke hearts from Trader Joe's. Otherwise I followed the recipe as written, and it came out perfectly. It was delicious and smelled divine! I think the crispy, chewy texture of the fried artichokes definitely added to the dish; next time I might try cooking them in my air fryer. The herb/capers/lemon zest topping was also great!

For example, in Spanish-speaking cultures they’re called “garbanzo beans”, and in Italian and Sicilian-speaking cultures they’re known as “ceci beans” (as in this recipe).  Other cultures have their own names, especially depending upon the type of chickpea.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Ceci Bean Soup With Red Pepper Pesto Recipe
commentBody:
This soup was quite delicious! I did make a number of adaptations because it was rather late when I started making it and I wanted to use what I had. To start I only used one can of beans because I am only one person also read through the comments on liquid and went with 2 cups broth, 4 cups water and that yielded a good consistency.  Also used garlic powder and minced onion for the first part. 

For the carrots and onions, I used onions. I pre-chopped and keep in the freezer did not thaw them just threw them right in. Last I did not have a pepper so I didn’t even make that part and it was quite fine without. Considering adding some chili crunch to spice it up or possibly a tomato pesto that I keep on hand at all times.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Ceci Bean Soup With Red Pepper Pesto Recipe
commentBody:
I really liked this soup and followed the comments about increasing the water. I used about twelve cups and the color came out close to the photo. Using dried beans reminded me how much tastier, if less convenient, they are than canned. 

The beans take quite a while to cook, and I didn’t really understand the point of waiting until they were done and draining them and reserving the liquid so you could cook the onion and carrots in the same pot and then adding the beans and reserved broth back in. So while the beans were cooking I sautéed the veggies in a separate pan. Once the beans were done I fished out the aromatics and just added the veggies to the pot. It seemed quicker, easier and delicious, but maybe I’m missing an important point. 

I used the immersion blender and puréed about half the beans and veggies to be creamy but still retain texture. As I am not a big fan of cayenne pepper, I substituted garlic in the pesto and it came out great. I tripled the peppers and added three garlic cloves so there would be plenty for the leftover soup.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Blood Orange Gelato Recipe
commentBody:
Does anyone else find it super frustrating to look at the reviews/comments and find that almost no one has actually made the recipe?  I don’t mean to sound harsh, but maybe folks can find another forum for reminiscing, random comments or speculation about how the recipe might work?  I’d like to read reviews that actually evaluate the recipe, and find it tiresome to sort through all the random notes.

Anyway, I DID make the recipe exactly as written, in an ice cream maker, and it turned out delicious.  It was also quite easy.  I didn’t have any problem with the addition of corn starch (contrary to one commenter’s notes, the amount IS specified as 2 TBSP, at least in the recipe that I am reading).  I used blood orange juice that I squeezed (to my surprise, I actually did need five of the oranges), as well as the zest of the blood oranges/lemon and Cointreau.  The blood orange flavor was pronounced.  I would make this again (I might try it with another citrus, although the blood oranges do have a unique taste and give it a beautiful pink tint).

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Glazed Pork Tenderloin With Gingery Carrots Recipe
commentBody:
This was fantastic. It is like the perfect blend of a Chicago deep dish and a 1980s Pizza Hut Supreme Pizza. My favorite homemade pizza recipe by far.

I read a lot of the comments and have to say, this recipe works as is. Although I recommend going with a whole can of tomatoes, not just a cup, and a whole package of mozz as well (although low moisture is essential! There’s a bit of water moisture management)
Make sure you brown the sausage, not cook it through initially, as I suspect the commenter who said their sausage was “carbonized” did. 
The crust does in fact recall a Dutch baby, despite another commenter’s passionate disagreement. This is mostly due to how eggy it is, which frankly was fine with my family and my young kids who are picky. 
This pizza is best eaten with a fork and a knife. Hard for me to say as a New Yorker!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Glazed Pork Tenderloin With Gingery Carrots Recipe
commentBody: We first made this and I just sautéed the carrots and ginger in butter and they browned, and ginger got this delicious caramelization on the carrots and crispness on the ginger. We made it again and I realized I didn’t follow it properly so I followed the recipe with the water and found the carrots to be disappointing and too soft. To be fair, we like a little crunch remaining in our cooked carrots and while the sauté version didn’t have much crunch since they were so thinly cut, they weren’t mushy. We will make this again, but will stick to the sauté without the water for a nice caramelization.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Matcha-Coconut Custard Recipe
commentBody:
Inspired first NYT Cooking comment!

I used 1.5 cans of unsweetened coconut milk; substituted date syrup for granulated sugar; interpreted "just below simmering" as 175 degrees; and was too lazy to strain.  Had creamy, smooth, bright green custard for breakfast after overnight in refrigerator.  Next week I will experiment with boxed coconut milk and coquito flavors (star anise, cinnamon, cloves and nutmeg).  My guess is that the gelatin will deal with either canned or boxed coconut milk.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Matcha-Coconut Custard Recipe
commentBody: I tried this recipe and it failed in two ways.  One I learned later from another recipe that Matcha gets bitter when you add it to a substrate that is too hot.  And secondly, even though I fallowed the recipe religiously it never congealed.  There was a hard crust on the top made of coconut fat but the main "pudding" was liquid even after letting it sit overnight.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Matcha-Coconut Custard Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe and just used a touch less sugar (90 instead of 100 grams). I think it could definitely be reduced more, as suggested by other commenters. Also I used nearly two cans of full fat coconut milk from Trader Joe’s for reference. First time using gelatin, and it kind of turned me off the final product! Had a weird smell that reminded me it’s made of hooves haha. For someone who loves matcha and coconut, I was very surprised (and saddened) to not enjoy the final product at all! To me, it was cloying and artificial tasting. I’ve had better succeed getting my matcha dessert fix via creme brûlée. Best of luck to other cooks!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Spiced Apple Fritters With Hot Honey Recipe
commentBody: Well I had no cast iron pan, Dutch oven or thermometer & did not want to deep fry. We don't do that much here in health conscious Nor Cal! Thought I could wing it. Used canola oil to fry, not deep fry. Used deep sided steel stock pot & guessed at the temp. They burned & blackened quickly. Kept turning temp down, still blackened quickly w some raw batter inside. But, I poured some maple syrup on them & ate them anyway! The idea is great, my approach could succeed w grated apples & better pot.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Black-Eyed Peas With Pork Belly and Greens Recipe
commentBody:
Nice recipe. Pressure cooking/Instant Pot works well for dried peas (20-25 mins pressure+natural release). 
  For vegans: Mashing at least half the peas after cooking (plus crushed tomatoes) adds umami. An Indian (Mughlai) recipe uses loads of browned onion with tomato, ginger, garlic, garam masala, mild chilies, garnished with chopped cilantro (or parsley for cilantro haters).
  I would serve the greens on the side. (Raw mustard greens make a great salad: a spicier, cheaper sub for arugula.)

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Black-Eyed Peas With Pork Belly and Greens Recipe
commentBody: Made this for New Year’s Day. But followed the photo, not the recipe. I am surprised no one else commented on the obvious — the pork belly in the photo is almost one inch crisped chunks. The recipe says to chop the pork into 1/2 chunks then crisp before adding to the stew. Duh, major disconnect here. The food stylist took way too many liberties and the editors did not catch the disparity. To achieve the result in the photo (which is ultimately a grand version of its humble roots,) cut the pork belly into larger chunks and crisp in a cast iron skillet.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Black-Eyed Peas With Pork Belly and Greens Recipe
commentBody:
Way too fussy and barely southern at all.

To do southern, use frozen peas if you can get 'em -- Whole Foods carries them. Southerners like 'em fresh, but not available in winter.

Add onion, carrot, and celery if ya like, otherwise just the peas and water (or preferably BROTH), some salt and pepper, and a smoked pork hock. 

Appropriate greens would be collards, cooked separately in broth with another hock. And for good luck, serve with hog jowls. UH -- no thanks -- carve up a ham.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Curd Roulade Recipe
commentBody:
When I make pumpkin rolls and cocoa rolls I find you get a nicer shape to the roll if you place a damp tea towel on a flat surface and turn the sheet pan onto it. Carefully remove the parchment and roll the cake up from the short side with the towel. 
Refrigerate for 3-4 hours for best results before filling. 
(I actually have 4 dedicated linen towels for this that I wash separately without perfumed detergent or softener and store in a plastic bag until the next time!)

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Curd Roulade Recipe
commentBody: My biggest gripe with this recipe is that it isn’t a roulade. This is more like a super inflated under stuffed Swiss Roll. The giveaway is the sponge. A roulade is a roll done with meringue. Use a sponge and it becomes a Swiss Roll. As far as Swiss Rolls go the pictures show a poor example. A good Swiss Roll has a thin layer of sponge and a healthy amount of stuff inside. Typically the curd should be melded with a cream of some kind.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Meatballs With Lentils and Greens Recipe
commentBody:
Another great Tanis dish.  Used lamb for meatballs. Lentils & greens would be good solo.  Made as written, notes by step #:
#1:  Smoked paprika is a lot.  Consider reduction.  Meat mix easier to handle when refrigerated several hours. May not need all the panade (milk bread). 
#4: Leeks take longer than 6 minutes to softened & light brown. Don't rush this.
#6: Let meatballs come to room temp (@ 60 min) before roasting & still took more than 15 min to done. (Tested w/insta read thermometer).

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Pear and Radicchio Salad With Blue Cheese Recipe
commentBody: Note to Meems re substituting olives for cheese -- It's not just saltiness that the cheese adds to the salad but creaminess. There are vegan cheeses that might work for you, or you might consider blending up something creamy (e.g. firm tofu or raw, soaked cashews) with something a little salty with a little umami (e.g., white miso). You could lightly dot this or drizzle it on the finished salad to keep your meal kosher. Different flavor profile than with cheese, but might be just as tasty.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Pear and Radicchio Salad With Blue Cheese Recipe
commentBody: @MML:  Same for me, and with ALL recipes, not only NYT. I have finally figured out that the timing reckoned for printed recipes appears always to be reckoned as the amount of time it would take on a cooking show or video—where all the prep (the “mise en place”) is already done. I’m persuaded that recipe writers never intend to include the time needed to pull all the ingredients out of storage, then measure, wash, peel, chop—all of that. That time is considerable!

commentBody:
This recipe is NOT a keeper. The crust is tough, hard, resistant to anything approach a neat cutting and not quite a jaw breaker to eat but close compared to a traditional butter pie crust.  The egg wash adds little. Beware to not burn the caramelized filling.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: French Fisherman Stew (La Chaudrée) Recipe
commentBody:
I've been a recreational fisherman for 40 years, and cook my catches, this recipe has some issues in my opinion:
1 - Use fish stock > fish bouillon > clam broth instead of water, in that order of preference.
2 - The fish should be cut larger than 1X1" - it WILL break.  
3 - The fish does not need 10 minutes of cook time. Add fish; as soon as the broth returns to a boil add the mussels and COVER the pot. When they open in around 3-4 minutes then add the calamari for 60-90 seconds.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: French Fisherman Stew (La Chaudrée) Recipe
commentBody:
The fish name 'rockfish' is used regionally to describe different fish species. In the mid-Atlantic region it's striped bass, but it's not a term fisherman in NY or New England generally use. Snapper is an equally misused/useless term.

What you want is a white fish on the thick side with a firm texture. 

If you're making this in the fall in the northeast look for 'Blackfish' aka Tautog - there is no finer soup for fish or chowders and tis the season - see also Monkfish.

Guessing 2-3 min is more accurate.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Plum-Cardamom Upside-Down Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I made the cake as directed. The plums I was gifted were small, so I halved them rather than slicing smaller. I used Italian plums. Three issues: I used melted butter as directed but my sugar, butter, eggs never got fluffy. Perhaps use softened butter instead. The brown sugar at the bottom of my pan leaked and burned at the bottom of the oven. Maybe my spring form pan is leakier than others? Knowing I should put a pan underneath to catch the drips would have been nice. Finally, after 45 minutes the top was so brown I took it out to check it. The toothpick came out clean. The toothpick lied. The middle of the cake fell as it cooled and is not fully cooked. If I had known that I would have just used foil to prevent more browning and put it in back in the oven. I'm not serving it to company so I can just cut around that bit. 
The cake is pretty and tastes good. It's a very sweet cake so some whipped cream or ice cream may be in order.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Plum-Cardamom Upside-Down Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I added about a Tsp cinnamon with the cardamom, and per other suggestions, put parchment down before the brown sugar/plums. It was really really good! It did take longer for the center to be done, but we may have beat it with the hand mixer too long. 
One idea for those that are saying it fell in the middle: When it says to sift the flour/baking powder/cardamom/salt together, I put those together all in a large measuring cup (or bowl or whatever you have). Then I sifted the flour mixture into the batter (Instead of sifting it into one container then dumping it into the batter as written, I actually sifted into the batter). Then all you need to do is give some good stirs & fold it all together. That may have helped keep it light and from collapsing. 
Who really knows. Baking is a fickle science! Especially in the dry high elevation Mountain West where I am!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Plum-Cardamom Upside-Down Cake Recipe
commentBody:
Nell, we've made the plum torte dozens and dozens of times, with all kinds of stone fruit. (My favorites are pluots and nectarines [separately or together] but YMMV.)

If your fruit didn't get cooked enough, I'd suggest double-checking the original recipe and making sure the oven is pre-heated. We've occasionally had very ripe fruit that soaked into the cake (for which the solution is to bake a little bit longer). Also, we use half or less the recommended amount of sugar, but YMMV.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Plum-Cardamom Upside-Down Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I used large plums that were called apricot plums or something like that. I let them ripen in room temperature for a week. Then I peeled them:  boiled them for 4-5 minutes and immersed in cold water after that. The peels came off easily. Then I soaked the slices in lemon juice while making the cake. 
I reduced the brown sugar to a good 1/2 cup. Spread that in the pan and put the plum wedges on top. I also reduced the sugar in the cake by almost half. The cake was beautiful and delicious.

commentBody:

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody:
So much fuss in the comments here!

I marinated the chicken in a bowl, bunged it on a sheet pan with the lemon slices--and (yikes!) unpeeled garlic--sprinkled the rosemary and some chili flakes over it (I use chili flakes like pepper if I'm roasting)

15 minutes in I added the squash to the side and stirred leeks into the chicken fat.

Cooked another 30 or so, sprinkled the parsley-lemon on and it was wonderful

(yeah I halved it to fit on one sheet pan)

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody: I don't understand why Tanis recommends rubbing spices into the chicken skin and then dousing the thighs in lemon juice. Won't the juice and later addition of oil wash off the spices? I added the spices to the lemon juice along with a little olive oil and marinated the chicken in a bowl. I sliced two lemons and scattered them with some smashed (peeled) garlic. I subbed thick slices of shallots for the leeks (unavailable). On a sheet pan, everything was cooked in less than an hour.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody: I used two sprigs of rosemary on the chicken (a quarter-cup, max) and a half-sprig on the squash. One head-equivalent of garlic, peeled, went between the thighs. I only had one leek, so substituted a yellow onion, sectioned. We preferred the onion to the leek in the finished dish. One sliced lemon sufficed. I used bottled fresh lemon juice. A quarter cup would have been enough. I also added chunks of peeled Russet potato. They soaked up the juices on one side and roasted on the other. Delicious!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made it 2x. The first exactly as written and it was delicious. Then, I made it with pressed tofu with a couple of modifications. First, I made a marinade. I put the lemon juice, spices, peeled garlic, salt, pepper, and rosemary into a mini blender. I put the mixture into a ziplock with the pressed tofu for about an hour. Baking pan had lemon slices with leeks, etc, but baked at 400 for 40 minutes. Used butternut squash (with sage) & put baking sheet in lower shelf under tofu pan for 35 minutes. It was really tasty!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody: I assembled the entire recipe, leaving it to marinade for several hours.  I substituted the rosemary for  Burlap and Barrel’s herbs de Provence and sprinkled twice as much olive oil on but half the lemon juice.  A roasting pan the size of the chicken thighs, rather than a sheet pan keeps the juices and marinade in resulting in a very moist and flavorful dish. It was roasted at 400 for about 50 minutes.  Quite delicious and will go into my favorites folder.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody: I have made this 3 times. Beautiful dish with delicata squash festooning the plate, and deep lemon flavor. I noticed that guests hesitated to touch the roasted garlic so, the most recent time, I squeezed it out before serving and passed sliced baguette to spread the garlic. I have a Meyer lemon tree so I use lots of lemons. I marinate an hour in a bowl, cook on a cookie sheet, make earlier in the day, refrigerate until dinnertime and reheat at 350. The dish pairs well with a good Pinot Noir.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody:
The flavors are delicious including the 1/2 cup of rosemary. The delicata squash was also delicious. 

However, I made adjustments. They did not work well. As follows: I used 3 lbs. boneless skinless thighs, peeled garlic, regular size leeks cut 1-1/2” long but still circular & reduced the lemon juice to 1/3 cup. Roasted in a 2-1/2 “ deep roasting pan for 60 minutes at 400 degrees F, the thigh temperature was 160 to 180 F, there was about 1/2 inch of liquid in the bottom of the pan, the garlic was still hard, the leeks were still hard. To cope, I poured the liquid, leeks and garlic into a saucepan & boiled to reduce liquid & soften the veg. The leeks cooked enough but the garlic only softened somewhat. The lemon slices never browned, they stewed. 

What did I do wrong? I’m guessing that the garlic needs to stay unpeeled, the leeks need to be less than 1 inch diameter. I don’t know why I had so much liquid at the end.  I would love to hear idea’s about that. Thanks.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Not a great recipe.   The flavors don’t meld together and the lemon shows its bitter side in this approach, while the papery garlic cloves and the huge rosemary leaves create texture issues at the table.  And I worked with outstanding ingredients: fresh farmer chicken that was never frozen, organic leeks and squash directly from a regional organic producer, home grown rosemary… I followed the instructions exactly, too.

Fundamentally, this recipe is trying to do something that would work perfectly well in a Le Creuset with a lot less lemon, or using desalted preserved lemon.  The idea was clearly to do a winter vegetable dish (roast chicken with leeks and squash), but then deconstruct it and introduce Moroccan spices to get a Raj al Hamout effect.  It fails because the entire concept doesn’t hold up well on a baking sheet at 400F.

Very sorry, but for me this was a classic example of an inadequately tested dish that was inspired by ones that work.  You really need to try your own recipes a bit more systematically.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Terrific flavors, and I’ll definitely make it again, but there are a few odd notes in the instructions. 

It calls for six lemons, one of which is zested but then not juiced; that’s a waste. I found that only four lemons were needed; one zested then juiced, one juiced, two sliced. It also tells you to seed the delicata twice, once before slicing it into full rings when it’s still whole (good luck with that!) and again after slicing. Leaving the delicata in rings effectively cuts the amount of squash you can roast on one pan in half; cutting the rings into half moons fixes that. The skins of our delicata were also unpleasantly hard, possibly because there wasn’t enough olive oil on them. 

Leaving such a large amount of rosemary leaves whole on top of the dish meant there were large, sharp, unpleasant spears of herbs in every bite. A smaller amount should be minced and added to the marinade, with whole sprigs placed underneath the chicken with the lemon slices. And marinating the chicken on a sheet pan instead of a bowl minimizes the depth of flavor in service of not washing one mixing bowl.

recipeTitle: Flan de Coco (Coconut Flan) Recipe
commentBody: You can make your own sweetened condensed coconut milk by combining a 13.5 oz. can of coconut milk and 1/4 cup of sugar in a saucepan. Over medium heat, mix together and bring to a low boil. Once it starts boiling, reduce the heat and let the mixture simmer rapidly for about 45 minutes, stirring frequently. It will thicken as it cools. Best to do it the day before.

commentBody: I was invited to a dinner and asked to bring a gluten free dessert. This fit the bill.  I had to make it twice as the first time I used evaporated milk by accident (it was sitting in my pantry.) It actually came out ok- but lacked sugar since evaporated milk is unsweetened. On the second round I used all the right ingredients but substituted sweetened condensed regular milk instead of sweetened condensed coconut milk since I couldn’t find it in the store.  It was a winner. Everyone loved it and most had seconds. I will put this in my rotation as it was an easy win.  We are enjoying the failed attempt and eating it for breakfast!

commentBody: Just made this and am so happy to have a dairy free flan!! You can get sweetened condensed coconut milk online if you don’t have local Asian markets. I found the batter be too sweet for my taste so I add a small can of cream of coconut. (NOTE: VIP to make sure that none of the coconut products you use have thickeners added). Also, following other reader suggestions, I added a teaspoonful of vanilla and a little bit of cinnamon. The sweetness level and texture were absolutely perfect. This can feed a crowd because it’s so rich. I even made a mess of my caramel when I poured it in my pie pan and it still came out wonderfully!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Avocado-Black Bean Tostadas Recipe
commentBody:
These were delicious.  I did find baking the soft corn tortillas didn’t make them crunchy enough to easily cut and had to resort to picking these up and eating them with my hands.  

I appreciated the tip from Gale to pour the boiling pickling liquid over the onions and jalapeños vs. cooking them in it.  I loved the touch of cinnamon it leant to them.  I also only used a teaspoon of sugar and salt vs. a tablespoon in the brine.

I sliced the avocados so they lay nicely on the refried black beans and I cubed the queso fresco to get a nice salty bite as one eats the finished tostada.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody: I made it last night and I wanted to say, as other commenters from the region of the recipe have mentioned, this recipe is authentic. I wanted to also add that it's just very well written. These  generational household dishes are almost like creative writing assignments (open-ended) passed down from cook to cook and creating a list of steps to recreate them requires talent. I followed the steps and got the desired dish that was promised. This can be hit and miss with such recipes. For those who are criticizing the recipe, I would say that it's not the recipe but the dish itself is just one that wasn't to your tastes and that's fine, we all have foods we like and dislike. So happy this recipe was written, thank you Madhur Jaffery.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody:
I am a huge fan of Madhur Jaffrey! I was able to meet her when I took a cooking class in London and have all her cookbooks and just love Indian food so much sadly, my husband does not but this recipe was perfect. He loved it. My kids loved it. It’s easy it takes no time at all and not spicy but good flavor. I doubled the amount of shrimp just because I have hungry eaters and I served it with jasmine rice and fresh, chopped cilantro and wedges of limes. 
This is a perfect recipe for those people who aren’t into curries, but you want to get them into curries. My husband is not a fan but he loved this recipe. He asked when I was gonna make it again and my kids loved it. It’s amazing quick easy perfect for a busy school night. .

commentBody:
Wonderful recipe! Added a tablespoon of tamarind concentrate to the paste when preparing. Threw in a bit of ground coriander too. If you do these steps, definitely taste before adding the lemon - tamarind is sour!

FYI - the sauce is even better if you prep in advance. I prepped the sauceearly afternoon, refrigerated, then reheated and added the shrimp - it was amazing!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody: In Goa, people hardly cook with lemon. The sour is got using Kokum or tamarind (raw mango when in season). Jaffrey does realize that her western audience do not keep Kokum or Tamarind or raw mango in their pantry and may find it difficult to get, so she uses Lemons. The only Goan ingredient here is Coconut milk which is present in most South Indian cooking.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody:
Fab easy party dish. Double like a dream. 
1. Use shrimp shells and tails: infuse them in the coconut mike for 30 minutes on a low simmer. 
2. Toast spices well in the onion mix. 
3. Cilantro stems and roots, ( if your cilantro comes with) tied into gauze can be thrown in when the coconut milk is added, or with the coconut/shrimpbits to infuse. 
4. Add some fresh spinach at the last minute. Again, that’s just me. 
5. I use lime rather than lemon.
6. Serve with cilantro lime rice. Toast naan and a radicchio salad with orange, almonds and fresh herbs tossed with olive oil and Maldon or other flake Salk.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody: Purists may cringe, but I had only a half-pound of shrimp. Sauteed 4 oz. cubed paneer with the shallots for extra protein then made remainder of recipe as written (plus stirred in 2 teaspoons tamarind sauce). Plus disproved the myth that dairy and seafood can't mix - the sauce was equally kind to all ingredients and the total was more than a sum of its parts.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody:
We loved this, with a few changes/additions (thank you to other commenters for your suggestions!). Very easy, quick recipe.

- 1 lb frozen thawed shrimp
- add 2 thawed frozen ginger cubes and 3 cloves chopped garlic to the shallots after 2 minutes
- then added 1 tsp curry powder, 1/2 tsp garam masala to recipe spices. (Used hot smoked paprika and 1/4 tsp cayenne. It was not too spicy.)
- 1 1/4 C coconut milk was right amount
- lay several stalks cilantro, tied, to steep with the coconut milk mixture.
- added 3-4 handfuls spinach along with shrimp
- lime juice instead of lemon
- served with jasmine rice

commentBody: Usually when I look at a curry recipe, it has a long list of ingredients I've never heard of, so I just order take-away. A suggestion was made below to include stir-fried green beans, which I'll try next time.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
commentBody:
Both nearby stores were out of full fat coconut milk, so I reluctantly settled for “lite,” which I normally find flavorless and abhorrent (think non-fat milk -  what’s the point?). Once it reduced along with the well-bloomed spices in the oil and a splash of shrimp stock, it was fine - sort of. Meaning, the spicy heat was quite pronounced due to the lack of richness to distribute and temper it; but we are pepper bellies, so it was delicious anyway, and we cleaned the pan. Next time I will hold out for full fat coconut milk, especially if serving others besides my husband and myself. 
Overall winner, and fast once shrimp are cleaned and mise en place is set up. Make this!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Gelo di Melone (Sicilian Watermelon Pudding) Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe turned out pretty well after a couple of modifications. 
1) The amount of sugar in the recipe is the minimum required, ie if your watermelon is insanely sweet. So, be prepared to add up to 50% more sugar.
2) Add a pinch or two of salt. It elevates the sweet flavor.
3) I must have used 50-60g of cornstarch since mine didn't dissolve completely (my fault).
4) I added 3T of cold butter at the end (like you would add in a lemon curd). Consistency is that of a curd as well.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Gelo di Melone (Sicilian Watermelon Pudding) Recipe
commentBody:
A wonderful recipe for an unexpected summer dessert, and dead easy, especially using cubed watermelon from the produce section. I’ve made this a few times now, and agree with others that the level of sugar should depend on the sweetness of your watermelon, so taste it and definitely add more sugar if your fruit isn’t super-sweet. Maybe it’s my stove, but I consistently need more than 2-3 minutes for the mixture to thicken when cooking, more like 7 minutes. (It’s  kinda fun when it happens, because within several seconds the mixture moves from a loose liquid to something velvety.) 

David’s note about a few drops of rosewater is spot on, but err on the side of caution. I was a little too liberal with the rosewater on my first try, and ended up with something that was more of a rose dessert than a watermelon dessert.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Gelo di Melone (Sicilian Watermelon Pudding) Recipe
commentBody: Knocked Back (about sixty years)! My grandmother in the Middle East used to make buttery crescent cookies filled with Turkish Delight. She would bake for weeks in anticipation of my arrival. Once I got started, I couldn’t stop— so much so, that she stored them under lock & key! I flavored my dessert with rose water and served it with fresh whipped cream topped with pistachios. Last, but not least with ice water in a tall aluminum tumbler (just like the ones I remember). Thanks for the memories, Chef! Delicious.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Pesto Pasta With Corn and Green Beans Recipe
commentBody:
Great, easy, delicious way to use up all that summer garden produce!  I had garlic scape and basil pesto on hand, so didn't follow that part of the recipe.  I put a bit of parsley into the corn sautee so the dish would have that flavor, too.  The finished product could use salt (the reason people may have called it "bland") and possibly a tiny lemon squeezy at the end.  
BTW: I subbed sugar snap peas for beans, cut into 2" pieces.  Worked like a charm!

recipeTitle: Pesto Pasta With Corn and Green Beans Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! In the spirit of full disclosure, I used this recipe as a base. I had some homemade pesto already on hand— just enough for 8 ounces of spaghetti (3-4oz). Because I always appreciate the comments, I toasted some breadcrumbs in butter and garlic, and used those as a topping. Brilliant! Added a nice, salty crunch. Because I love everything lemony—especially herby and vegetal dishes—I squeezed in a good amount. I loved it! It’s seasonal & satisfying w/o being heavy. Will make again!

recipeTitle: Tomato-Pepper Salad With Tapenade Recipe
commentBody:

This is a great recipe for jumping off for me.

commentBody: Wow, why have I never made my own olive tapenade?  This is a great recipe!Very straightforward so much so that I could throw it together after a long afternoon of kayaking. author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody:
Made with a few amendments and it was very delicious, a great summer recipe! 
Amendments:
- halved the recipe, as that was still a plenty big serving (for 5, we still had about 1/3 leftovers)
- replaced some rum with amaretto since I hadn’t had any almond extract 
- approximately halved sugar - we don’t like it so sweet and peaches were so sweet and delicious 
- just added a little sugar zu the peaches instead of jam
- used as many ladyfingers as needed to make 2 solid cake layers - this amounted to about double the lady fingers recommended per recipe 
-> would peel the peaches next time, even if everyone said it was fine, I didn’t like it so much
-> I would opt for yellow peaches next time

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This is my new favorite summer dessert. I modified this recipe the second time I made it and preferred it:
1. Omit the ricotta. For me it was a texture issue.
2. Instead of ladyfingers, I used Trader Joe’s French Galette Butter Cookies.
3. I salted my peaches. 
4. I used only 2 Tbsp of jam (I cut the recipe ingredients in half for 4-5 servings).
5. A bread loaf pan works well for 4-5 servings (1/2 recipe ingredients).

So good!

commentBody:
Regarding the interest on non-alcohol syrup.

The wine can be replaced with some white grape juice plus some water, and maybe a few drops of lemon juice.

commentBody: I was really looking forward to this dessert, however after making it, I think this recipe needs to be rethought. The ratios are all out of whack. Too few ladyfingers for this amount of cream. Too much peaches. It did not fit into a 9-inch pan at all. Three pounds of sliced peaches alone would barely fit I to a pan this size. And the flavors were flat. I spent a lot of money on excellent peaches, good quality dairy, and imported ladyfingers. No one enjoyed it. The peaches absolutely should be peeled/deskinned otherwise it’s a mess to cut through them. I’d advise dicing the peaches rather than slicing and macerating with 1-2T of honey and a bit of cinnamon or other warming spice along with the recipe’s jam ingredient. The cream wasn’t sweet enough. I’d also add a dash of cardamom or cloves for flavor depth and excitement. So much went uneaten into the yard waste, a real disappointment. Sorry David Tanis. I’m a fan, but this one goes back to the test kitchen.

recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody: We used fresh local peaches and made a half peach & half ginger syrup as we had neither wine nor spirits. The mise en place took us a while. I do suggest future recipes put the recommended utensils and pans in a list rather than embed them in the recipe. I prefer the yield be included with the times.  Perhaps everything that will be divided could be identified in the ingredients. Finally, I understand serving yields are a guide, but 8-10 servings from a 9x9 pan is, shall we say, challenging.

recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I make many versions of tiramisu during the summer using whatever fresh fruit is available. 
When I make peach versions I use Distilleries de Provence Rinquinquin a La Peche Liqueur France as the "syrup" just adding a touch of sweetener.  
I have found that the Goya brand of dried lady fingers works perfectly and are often half the price of imported or "Italian" style options.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic!  Only problem is I correctly though to buy a second pkg of cookies and I needed 4 beyond the first pkg with about 1 “ cut off 3 of them.  Also, plots of peaches left and the whip cream mixture. I “sold” most of the extra peaches by leaving them out and letting people decide if they wanted more peaches.  The extra whip cream was never going to fit in my 8”x 8” sq pan.  A future solution would be to use a 9x13 pan, 2 pkgs cookies, and only one layer of the crème fraiche but everything else as recipe dictates.  We had 9 generous servings as is but the 9x13 pan would have served 12-14. I would make this again in a heartbeat!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made changes: Made the syrup as per the recipe. Layered the bottom of a 9-inch pan with dipped ladyfingers. Covered that with 15 oz of lightly sweetened, whipped ricotta (used food processor). Covered that with a layer of sliced peaches mixed with jam. Covered that with another layer of ladyfingers. Topped with cream: I used 1.5 cups of whipping cream, two tbsp. of powdered sugar, and 4 oz. of mascarpone, whipped until fairly stiff. Chilled. Ditched the créme fraîche completely.

In the fridge now awaiting the taste test.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody: We and our company loved the dessert - the key was local peaches!  Thank you Chiles peach orchard in Crozet, VA.  As I looked at the recipe I was concerned that a typical 9 in pan would not be deep enough. This came to prove true…it’s also not clear what constitutes a ‘lady ginger’.  Our packages of lady fingers contain 12 split fingers.  I decided a finger was both halves BUT 12 would not cover a 9 inch pan…I used a 10 inch springform pan (yes it leaked, definitely put it in a lipped jelly roll pan), a layer of half fingers (24) lightly sprinkled with the sugar sauce (I knew it would leak down into the layer).

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
commentBody:
9x9 pans are uncommon — my baking dish is 8x8 — so I made this in a 9.5 inch deep dish pie plate.  It is about 10 sq inches (71 vs 81) smaller than 9x9.  It would be great if the Times used common sizes!
However, the heaping conglomeration won rave reviews.   Fresh SC peaches from the local farmers market were key.  Don’t bother if your peaches don’t cause instant states of bliss.  I used fresh cream from a very local dairy and bakery lady fingers.  The booze didn’t hurt either.

commentBody:

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Chilled Cucumber-Spinach Soup  Recipe
commentBody: One of the reasons I admire David Tanis so much is his subtlety. In early summer when you have access to freshly picked cucumbers with dense, crisp flesh that were grown in fields vs. greenhouses the size of NJ, the delicate flavor of this soup is a pleasure. Climate change unfortunately means that your spinach will not have been harvested under the same conditions, but if it didn't come in a plastic clamshell you might pull off a nice bowl of soup. You can always grab a bag of Flaming Cheetos afterwards.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Chilled Cucumber-Spinach Soup  Recipe
commentBody:
Made it for lunch today and served in verines as a first course. I put it in the fridge to chill already served in verines and it went really fast. I served it 45 minutes later and it was chilled perfectly. 
For garnishes I just put Thai basil from my garden and a drizzle of roasted sesame oil. I skipped the soy sauce and the tofu since it was a first course and didn’t need the protein. If I had it as a main course however I would add the tofu but I think I’d still skip the soy sauce. The spinach flavor is so good that I don’t want to lose it.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Chilled Cucumber-Spinach Soup  Recipe
commentBody:
I would recommend this as a soup base but that's about it. More flavor please. Here's what I added and suggest:
- I started to strain it as listed, which resulted in "spinach water." I left all the fiber instead. 
- I add garlic powder, onion powder and it was still on the bland side.
- Next time I make this, I'll leave out the sesame oil and soy and finish with fresh tomatoes, diced cucumbers, sour cream or plain yogurt and a drizzle of olive oil. Think "spinach-based gazpacho."

commentBody:
This one wasn't what I was hoping for (love cold soups and gazpachos of all colors). In tasting as I prepped, the spinach-leek-pimeton mixture was really nice, but in straining it you loose all the texture a chilled soup has--I added in a few spoons of the strained spinach to try to course correct but couldn't bring it back. Final result was watery and not flavorful. 

The miso crab cakes that went along with this summer supper WERE really lovely though. Not all was lost ;)

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Chilled Cucumber-Spinach Soup  Recipe
commentBody:
The texture confused me. I cranked a Vitamix up to 8 (had to add a couple Tbs of water so it wouldn’t seize up), and still got a texture like finely chopped spinach. Straining was impossible unless I wanted about a cup of clear, spinach flavored cucumber water and many cups of solid discard.

Agree with others that the flavor is basic. Maybe ancho or chipotle chili powder instead? Green hatch powder, maybe? Or go in a different direction and try baharat or curry. I’m inexperienced with cold soups so I’m afraid to just chuck in garlic. I really wanted to like this but the flavor doesn’t make up for the texture.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Oven Chicken Kebabs Recipe
commentBody: I guess I will be the contrarian here.  I don’t have a “timid palate.”  When I tasted the results, I went back to look at the recipe to see if I had misread it and the tablespoons of cumin and pepper flakes were supposed to be teaspoons instead. The toasted ground cumin seeds and red-pepper flakes completely took over the flavor profile, and not in a good way.  So, I would cut those two ingredients in half for a better overall result.  I suspect that this comment will engender some “spice shaming” replies, but I’ll stick by my assessment that these spice proportions are out of whack.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Oven Chicken Kebabs Recipe
commentBody: I made one batch of chicken and one of lamb. I didn't have any sumac so I used lemon rind instead (internet suggested replacement) - about 2/3 tbsp. The lemon flavor was a little stronger than I would have liked but the skewers all got rave reviews from our guests. The lamb in particular. Next time I will probably drain the juice from the grated onion before adding so the raw mixture is a little less wet. Delicious and easy. Would make again.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
commentBody: I served the herbed cucumber-yogurt salad yesterday along with grilled beef kebabs and a couscous salad. It was delicous! This salad can’t be compared with tzatziki (I am not a big fan of). The texture of the thinly sliced cucumber and creamy yogurt but more important the taste of the mint, dill, lemon zest and juice (I did not add cayenne or chile) make this salad indeed to a „cooling, pleasently refreshing saucy accompaniment to grilled meat“. I will make it all summer long! Thank you David.

recipeTitle: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
commentBody: Slice/lightly salt cucumber/onion ahead and let drain.  Pre-mix the other ingredients.  Stir everything together shortly before serving.  Less chance of a watery salad. author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
commentBody: Flavors are great!  I didn’t have access to a chile, but did include the cayenne, although it was not noticeable, but I’ll increase next time.  The reason for the 4 stars was the amount of yogurt - I used the metric measurements and included the required grams of cucumber plus about 40 grams.  The recipe also calls for 3 cups of yogurt, which I also completed on grams according to the yogurt tub nutrition facts.  As I was adding it, I thought this is a lot of yogurt-I ended up adding 1.9 cups according to my calculations.  Unfortunately, it still a lot of yogurt.  It still tastes great, but it’s kinda a soupy mess of a cucumber salad as more water has been released by the cucumber because of the salt.  If that’s your jam, go for it, but I think I’ll do a third of the yogurt next time.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
commentBody: I read through the comments first.   Salted the cucumbers, squeezed the water, then rinsed off all the salt.  Maceration.  Onions I soaked in water making them mild. The extra fat yogurt was able to absorb the lemon juice so it wasn't runny.  Added honey to cut the acid.  Nice served in bibb lettuce bowl.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Try using Greek yogurt or Skyr. Even low fat versions of those can provide a thicker and creamier texture. 

This tastes good as a topping or dipping sauce for burritos, tacos, gyros, pita wraps, chopped salad greens, or mixed with beans or chickpeas… lots of options. 

I even like it on its own, as a topping on really good, crusty bread. By adding a little kefir or buttermilk to thin it, it can also be eaten with a spoon, as a cold soup. 

For vegans, this can be made with a thick, unsweetened vegan yogurt.

commentBody: I have an alternative method for cooking down greens. I prep them per the recipe wash & drain well, then zap them in the microwave, 2-3 minutes based on volume. This way not only can partial prep be done in advance, but the aromatics are not diluted & thrown out with excess liquid in the greens.

recipeTitle: Lamb Shoulder With Greens and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Easter dinner with boneless leg of lamb because that's what my store had. I did sear the meat very briefly at the start because I just couldn't resist. I covered the meat closely with baking parchment (crumpled & smoothed) before putting the lid on. Baked at 325 for first hour+, then at 250 for the rest of the 2 hours. Everybody loved it and all our guests wanted leftovers to take home. Peeling the favas and rinsing the greens (chard & Tuscan kale) took the most time.

recipeTitle: Lemon Cake With Strawberries and Cream Recipe
commentBody:
made this as I’m always on a hunt for a quick dessert for a potluck. Quite good. I think the next time, will do 1/4 t lemon extract and 1/4 t vanilla to up the lemon flavor. Over-folded when adding the flour which made the sponge a little tough. needed more than 8 oz of strawberries! 

It’s a good, easy recipe which can be pulled together, start to serving, in a little over 3 hrs. Perfect for those “can you bring a dessert?” invites!

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Cake With Strawberries and Cream Recipe
commentBody: I just relegated this cake to the garbage.  Thank goodness that I made it a day ahead.  Looked a bit counterintuitive but seemed quick and easy.  First, after 15 minutes whisking the mixture was thick but not holding peaks.  I forged  on nonetheless.  The next counterintuitive part: the recipe says to put the sifted flour in a large bowl and fold the liquid mixture into the dry ingredient. Despite my misgivings I went ahead.  The result was an unservable cake with a solid layer in the bottom.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Lemon Cake With Strawberries and Cream Recipe
commentBody: There was too much batter for the 9" springform pan. The batter rose up over the top and cascaded down to the cake strips, which stopped the flow. Next time (and there will be a next time), I'll use the 10". Very light and airy. Almost too light. I'm thinking I took the "almost to peaks' approach too far, as the batter definitely held its shape. There was no "slowly dissolving ribbon", to quote Julia Child. It baked to a clean skewer in 20 minutes.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Cabbage Salad With Sesame Recipe
commentBody: Christa, “lightly peeled” means, simply, that you don’t dig into the flesh of the cucumber when peeling. Just skim — “lightly” — the cuke with your peeler, leaving some strips of peel on the fruit. A hothouse cucumber has a tender skin that is usually eaten, while a field grown cucumber has something more akin to a soft rind, which is usually removed. In the photo, you can see that the cuke slices in the salad retain some rind. It adds to the bright color of the dish.

author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Cabbage Salad With Sesame Recipe
commentBody:
We live in a town that has a plethora of Asian grocery stores, so I was able to procure everything for this recipe but cilantro and dill, which I picked up at the regular supermarket. I have never used Napa cabbage and I am so glad I did.  It's so much more delicate than green cabbage. 
Followed recipe exactly and it was outstanding. I took it to a dinner at friends' and it was the tastiest thing on the table. 
Glad we loved it as I will be making it again this week to finish off the vegetables and herbs! Will follow others' advice to double the dressing and keep it on hand. It will be a great side salad for salmon or grilled chicken.

commentBody: This is very delicious and I will make it again for sure.. I made it with napa cabbage and next time I will change up how I put it together. author: David Tanis
recipeTitle: Spiced Chicken Breasts in Pastry Recipe
commentBody: The idea: moist chicken surrounded by pastry.  Years ago, I created  an analogue with far less work. Slice microwaved  seasoned breasts  into 1/4 - 1/3 slices.  Cut an oblong sourdough loaf x-wise alternately into 80% & nearly complete 1/4-1/3" thick slices. Slather insides of 80% cuts w. pesto, put chick slice in. Dribble a little melted butter over the loaf. Bake at 350 for 30 minutes. Guests pull apart ready made moist pesto chicken sandwiches! Now, sub pesto w Dijon, onion, cream, 5-spice...
